Open Source AI.title: How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI - Amazon Web Services
Open Source AI.titleZh: How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI - Amazon Web Services
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to Main Content](#aws-page-content-main) AWS Blogs * [Home](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/) ## [AWS Architecture Blog](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/) # How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI *This post is cowritten with Yuri Brigance, and Usman M. Khan from Aigen.* [Aigen](https://www.aigen.io/) builds autonomous robots designed to help farmers remove herbicide-resistant weeds and improve crop yield through AI-driven technology. These robots operate without chemicals, using renewable energy, and provide real-time, field-level data to enhance decision-making. Using advanced computer vision AI, Aigen’s robots autonomously identify and remove weeds without harming crops, giving farmers an eco-friendly, cost-effective solution to traditional weed management and efficient farming. As its robotic fleet expanded, Aigen’s on-premises infrastructure became a bottleneck in scaling its model-building pipeline. In this post, you will learn how Aigen modernized its machine learning (ML) pipeline with [Amazon SageMaker AI](https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/ai/?nc=sn&loc=1&refid=ceaf07a2-36ab-4fba-b62f-bcf6c48ca9f2) to overcome industry-wide agricultural robotics challenges and scale sustainable farming. This post focuses on the strategies and architecture patterns that enabled Aigen to modernize its pipeline across hundreds of distributed edge solar robots and showcase the significant business outcomes unlocked through this transformation. By adopting automated data labeling and human-in-the-loop validation, Aigen increased image labeling throughput by 20x while reducing image labeling costs by 22.5x. ## Key challenges of scaling field agricultural robots Aigen’s initial ML pipeline was designed to build task-specific edge models for its field robots. Robot data was uploaded to [Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3)](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) for manual labeling. The annotated datasets were then used to train new task-specific edge models on Aigen’s on-premises infrastructure. However, this ML pipeline introduced several limitations: * **Connectivity Constraints:** Inconsistent internet in rural areas hampered communication between robots and cloud. * **High Data Labeling Cost:** Manual data labeling of thousands of new samples data per day proved prohibitively expensive and time-consuming. * **Limited Computational Power:** Training specialized edge models and fine-tuning foundation models (FMs) for specific tasks using on-premises hardware was a bottleneck due to limited parallelism and GPU compute power with on-premises RTX 3090 machines. * **Scalability Issues:** Model Training and data labeling batch Inference had to compete for the same RTX 3090 machines, causing delays either for the data science team for model training or the data labeling team for batch inference. ## Solution Aigen addresses these challenges by adopting an AWS AI-driven, cloud-native approach that enables scalable and automated operations: * **Edge Computing:** Robots use AWS IoT Core and cloud utilities to safely offload data to Amazon S3, even in low-connectivity regimes. * **Automated Data Pipeline:** Data collected by the robots flows through an Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) pipeline for preprocessing. Data labeling is accelerated using an ensemble of vision foundation models (Grounding DINO, Owl-ViT, SAM2, CLIPSeg) along with custom expert vision models to automatically annotate large volumes of field imagery. Through active learning, the pipeline selects and down-samples the most informative samples, which are then reviewed and refined by human annotators before being passed downstream into the model training workflow. * **Cloud native ML Pipeline:** Aigen accelerates model training on Amazon SageMaker AI, using Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) across multi-GPU clusters to achieve faster iteration cycles and efficient hyperparameter tuning. By scaling training in the cloud, Aigen...
Open Source AI.contentZh: [Skip to Main Content](#aws-page-content-main) AWS Blogs * [Home](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/) ## [AWS Architecture Blog](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/) # How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI *This post is cowritten with Yuri Brigance, and Usman M. Khan from Aigen.* [Aigen](https://www.aigen.io/) builds autonomous robots designed to help farmers remove herbicide-resistant weeds and improve crop yield through AI-driven technology. These robots operate without chemicals, using renewable energy, and provide real-time, field-level data to enhance decision-making. Using advanced computer vision AI, Aigen’s robots autonomously identify and remove weeds without harming crops, giving farmers an eco-friendly, cost-effective solution to traditional weed management and efficient farming. As its robotic fleet expanded, Aigen’s on-premises infrastructure became a bottleneck in scaling its model-building pipeline. In this post, you will learn how Aigen modernized its machine learning (ML) pipeline with [Amazon SageMaker AI](https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/ai/?nc=sn&loc=1&refid=ceaf07a2-36ab-4fba-b62f-bcf6c48ca9f2) to overcome industry-wide agricultural robotics challenges and scale sustainable farming. This post focuses on the strategies and architecture patterns that enabled Aigen to modernize its pipeline across hundreds of distributed edge solar robots and showcase the significant business outcomes unlocked through this transformation. By adopting automated data labeling and human-in-the-loop validation, Aigen increased image labeling throughput by 20x while reducing image labeling costs by 22.5x. ## Key challenges of scaling field agricultural robots Aigen’s initial ML pipeline was designed to build task-specific edge models for its field robots. Robot data was uploaded to [Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3)](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) for manual labeling. The annotated datasets were then used to train new task-specific edge models on Aigen’s on-premises infrastructure. However, this ML pipeline introduced several limitations: * **Connectivity Constraints:** Inconsistent internet in rural areas hampered communication between robots and cloud. * **High Data Labeling Cost:** Manual data labeling of thousands of new samples data per day proved prohibitively expensive and time-consuming. * **Limited Computational Power:** Training specialized edge models and fine-tuning foundation models (FMs) for specific tasks using on-premises hardware was a bottleneck due to limited parallelism and GPU compute power with on-premises RTX 3090 machines. * **Scalability Issues:** Model Training and data labeling batch Inference had to compete for the same RTX 3090 machines, causing delays either for the data science team for model training or the data labeling team for batch inference. ## Solution Aigen addresses these challenges by adopting an AWS AI-driven, cloud-native approach that enables scalable and automated operations: * **Edge Computing:** Robots use AWS IoT Core and cloud utilities to safely offload data to Amazon S3, even in low-connectivity regimes. * **Automated Data Pipeline:** Data collected by the robots flows through an Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) pipeline for preprocessing. Data labeling is accelerated using an ensemble of vision foundation models (Grounding DINO, Owl-ViT, SAM2, CLIPSeg) along with custom expert vision models to automatically annotate large volumes of field imagery. Through active learning, the pipeline selects and down-samples the most informative samples, which are then reviewed and refined by human annotators before being passed downstream into the model training workflow. * **Cloud native ML Pipeline:** Aigen accelerates model training on Amazon SageMaker AI, using Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) across multi-GPU clusters to achieve faster iteration cycles and efficient hyperparameter tuning. By scaling training in the cloud, Aigen...

Infra & Chips.title: AI-RAN – lots of talk, little action, no guarantees - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.titleZh: AI-RAN——空谈多、行动少、毫无保障——Light Reading
Infra & Chips.content: * [5G](/wireless/5g) * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [6G](/wireless/6g) * [Semiconductors](/network-technology/semiconductors) * [News](/latest-news) # AI-RAN – lots of talk, little action, no guarantees When it comes to Nvidia's GPUs, keeping AI separate from RAN compute might be the most sensible approach for most telcos. [Iain Morris,](/author/iain-morris)International Editor,Light Reading March 30, 2026 6 Min Read Nvidia's GPUs continue to be hyped, but it's still tumbleweed in AI-RAN town.(Source: Nvidia) Thieves who steal copper wires from telecom networks have plagued operators for years. A graphics processing unit (GPU), one of the pricey chips made by Nvidia, could be another juicy target for criminals in the radio access network (RAN), fears Bruno Zerbib, the chief technology officer of Orange. Nvidia's most powerful GPUs each sell for tens of thousands of dollars, helping to explain why its gross profit margins are still north of 70%. At the base of a cell site, or in a nearby, unguarded small data center, they may look irresistible to the average crook. Prospective GPU snatchers might have to wait a few years, though. Acres of coverage about the concept of AI-RAN, popularized by Nvidia, might have created the impression that GPUs are sprinkled liberally across the world's 7 million mobile sites. In fact, you are more likely to unearth a working Nokia phone, a functional relic of the 1990s mobile boom. Nokia, the only big RAN vendor designing software for GPUs, does not expect to have commercial products ready until the end of this year, confirmed Pallavi Mahajan, its chief technology officer (CTO), at Nokia's MWC press conference a few weeks ago. No telco has announced plans for a commercial deployment. All the noise is about trials and most of it emanates from just two operators – T-Mobile US and Japan's SoftBank. The availability gap was noted by John Saw, the former's chief technology officer, at the same press conference. "I would be really upset if a year from now Pallavi didn't deliver an AI-RAN radio," [he told analysts and reporters](/5g/t-mobile-eager-to-test-nokia-s-ai-ran-this-year), sounding more confident that the Seattle Seahawks will win the Superbowl again next year (they beat the New England Patriots 29-13 at this year's final on February 8). Nokia CEO Justin Hotard was quick to interject, promising that Saw will not be disappointed. "That's definitely going to happen," said Hotard (and not about the NFL). "That one I can assure you." But meeting his delivery target for an AI-RAN product does not mean T-Mobile will quickly embark on a major commercial rollout, especially when there is no compelling evidence that having a GPU-based RAN would give the operator a big advantage over its rivals in the battle for customers. ##### Slowly does it SoftBank, the other notable cheerleader for AI-RAN, has seemingly moved slowly and with caution despite some of the enthusiastic-sounding headlines. "We have been working on this development, and we are aiming to deploy a small number of sites maybe within the next fiscal year, within this year or maybe early next year. That is the plan," said Ryuji Wakikawa, the vice president of SoftBank's advanced technology division, just over a year ago. Supporting commercial traffic is not the same as running a trial, requiring a "different level of quality and functions," he told reporters on a call before MWC 2025. "Hopefully, a few sites within 12 to 18 months, and we will decide how to deploy further in our network after that," he said at the time. T-Mobile US and SoftBank are not the only big operators linked with GPU-based AI-RAN interest. A year ago, Indonesia's Indosat Ooredoo Hutchison (IOH) [was reported by Computer Weekly](https://www.computerweekly.com/news/366620460/Indosat-Ooredoo-Hutchison-claims-AI-RAN-first-for-Southeast-Asia), part of the same group as Light Reading, to have kicked off a "landmark" initiative to buil...

30, 2026* ## Introduction As of early 2026, NVIDIA Corp. ([NASDAQ: NVDA](http://markets.chroniclejournal.com/chroniclejournal/quote?Symbol=NQ%3ANVDA)) has transcended its origins as a high-end graphics card manufacturer to become the undisputed architect of the global "Intelligence Economy." With a market capitalization fluctuating between $4.1 trillion and $4.4 trillion, Nvidia now rivals the GDP of major sovereign nations. This research feature explores how a single fabless semiconductor company achieved a valuation that dwarfs traditional manufacturing giants, driven by a relentless innovation cycle and a software-defined ecosystem that rivals the dominance of the internet's early protocols. ## Historical Background Founded in 1993 by Jensen Huang, Chris Malachowsky, and Curtis Priem, Nvidia initially focused on the niche market of 3D graphics for gaming. The company’s trajectory changed forever in 2006 with the launch of CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture). By allowing researchers to use GPUs for general-purpose mathematical calculations, Nvidia planted the seeds for the modern AI revolution. While the industry initially viewed CUDA as a distraction from gaming, it became the foundation for the Deep Learning breakthrough of 2012 (AlexNet) and the subsequent Generative AI explosion of 2023. Infra & Chips.title: VCs continue to pile into AI inference chip startups - pitchbook.com
Infra & Chips.titleZh: 风投继续涌入AI推理芯片初创公司 - pitchbook.com
Infra & Chips.content: AI & ML # VCs continue to pile into AI inference chip startups ## Chip startups focused on the AI workload are the new ‘recurring revenue’ business. [Rebellions](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/454235-23), a South Korean chipmaker, is at least the ninth company specializing in AI inference to announce new funding so far in 2026 as VCs continue to pile into the category. Investors’ interest is largely driven by a simple thesis: As AI moves from experimentation into real-world deployment, the infrastructure layer that serves output—inference—is shaping up to be a recurring-revenue business. “Inference represents this transition from experimental AI to the delivery of real-world value at a massive scale,” said Mo Jomaa, Partner at [CapitalG](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/investor/61551-91), which has invested in [Baseten](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/462191-14), an inference infrastructure startup. “The focus is rapidly shifting from training models to making them operational and accessible in order to power the next generation of AI-native applications.” The appeal to investors is structural. Unlike training AI models, which lasts a finite amount of time, inference is the meter running: every API call, agent action and enterprise query. And as companies and customers are increasingly demanding more from AI applications, the need for inference has only grown. “Once a product is ready and customers start using it, when it starts to scale, that’s when inference is needed,” said Assaf Araki, investment director at [Intel Capital](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/investor/11220-13), an investor in [SambaNova](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/226766-53), an AI inference hardware development startup. “It’s way more stable from a revenue perspective than development.” Inference startups are catching tailwinds from other hot companies like [Cursor,](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/519419-89) which develops a code editor, or legaltech startup [Legora](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/539201-26), gaining traction with enterprise clients. In early January, AI inference infrastructure startup [Inferact](https://pitchbook.com/profiles/company/1242942-04) announced it had raised $150 million in seed funding. Weeks later, in early February, Baseten unveiled its own $300 million round. Sign up for The Daily Pitch newsletter And as more companies selling AI services gain a foothold, the need for inference will continue to grow. “Inference is the core of every interaction with an AI product or model,” said Baseten CEO and co-founder Tuhin Srivastava. “Investing in inference is the same as investing in the growth and adoption of AI as a whole.” For certain markets, the race to provide inference-focused chips has created a new opportunity. South Korea, for example, in December established the National Growth Fund, a KRW 150 trillion (roughly $100 billion) effort to invest in companies, funds and other channels [to help fuel the country’s semiconductor industry](https://pitchbook.com/news/reports/2026-south-korea-private-capital-breakdown). It participated in Rebellions’ new funding round. Mistral first announced plans to build [a data center last year](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-02-10/mistral-ceo-plans-to-raise-more-funds-to-back-ai-data-center), when its CEO Arthur Mensch said it would explore different financing options in February 2025. It plans to complete building the data center in Bruyeres-le-Chatel and make it operational in the second quarter of 2026, Reuters reported on Monday. Mistral did not immediately return a request seeking confirmation. Last month, the company said it would invest [$1.4 billion in Sweden](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/11/mistral-ai-infrastructure-sweden.html) to build out AI infrastructure, including data centers. Mistral said it aims to deploy 200 megawatts of compute capacity across Europe by 2027. “Scaling our infrastructure in Europe is critical to empower our customers and to ensure AI innovation and autonomy remain at the heart of Europe. We will continue to invest in this area, given the surging and sustained demand from governments, enterprises, and research institutions seeking to build their own customized AI environment, rather than depend on third-party cloud providers,” Mensch said in a statement to CNBC. Mistral has raised over €2.8 billion ($3.1 billion) in funding to date from investors including General Catalyst, ASML, a16z, Lightspeed, and DST Global, according to data from Crunchbase. Infra & Chips.title: How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI - Amazon Web Services
Infra & Chips.titleZh: How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI - Amazon Web Services
Infra & Chips.content: [Skip to Main Content](#aws-page-content-main) AWS Blogs * [Home](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/) ## [AWS Architecture Blog](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/) # How Aigen transformed agricultural robotics for sustainable farming with Amazon SageMaker AI *This post is cowritten with Yuri Brigance, and Usman M. Khan from Aigen.* [Aigen](https://www.aigen.io/) builds autonomous robots designed to help farmers remove herbicide-resistant weeds and improve crop yield through AI-driven technology. These robots operate without chemicals, using renewable energy, and provide real-time, field-level data to enhance decision-making. Using advanced computer vision AI, Aigen’s robots autonomously identify and remove weeds without harming crops, giving farmers an eco-friendly, cost-effective solution to traditional weed management and efficient farming. As its robotic fleet expanded, Aigen’s on-premises infrastructure became a bottleneck in scaling its model-building pipeline. In this post, you will learn how Aigen modernized its machine learning (ML) pipeline with [Amazon SageMaker AI](https://aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/ai/?nc=sn&loc=1&refid=ceaf07a2-36ab-4fba-b62f-bcf6c48ca9f2) to overcome industry-wide agricultural robotics challenges and scale sustainable farming. This post focuses on the strategies and architecture patterns that enabled Aigen to modernize its pipeline across hundreds of distributed edge solar robots and showcase the significant business outcomes unlocked through this transformation. By adopting automated data labeling and human-in-the-loop validation, Aigen increased image labeling throughput by 20x while reducing image labeling costs by 22.5x. ## Key challenges of scaling field agricultural robots Aigen’s initial ML pipeline was designed to build task-specific edge models for its field robots. Robot data was uploaded to [Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3)](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) for manual labeling. The annotated datasets were then used to train new task-specific edge models on Aigen’s on-premises infrastructure. However, this ML pipeline introduced several limitations: * **Connectivity Constraints:** Inconsistent internet in rural areas hampered communication between robots and cloud. * **High Data Labeling Cost:** Manual data labeling of thousands of new samples data per day proved prohibitively expensive and time-consuming. * **Limited Computational Power:** Training specialized edge models and fine-tuning foundation models (FMs) for specific tasks using on-premises hardware was a bottleneck due to limited parallelism and GPU compute power with on-premises RTX 3090 machines. * **Scalability Issues:** Model Training and data labeling batch Inference had to compete for the same RTX 3090 machines, causing delays either for the data science team for model training or the data labeling team for batch inference. ## Solution Aigen addresses these challenges by adopting an AWS AI-driven, cloud-native approach that enables scalable and automated operations: * **Edge Computing:** Robots use AWS IoT Core and cloud utilities to safely offload data to Amazon S3, even in low-connectivity regimes. * **Automated Data Pipeline:** Data collected by the robots flows through an Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) pipeline for preprocessing. Data labeling is accelerated using an ensemble of vision foundation models (Grounding DINO, Owl-ViT, SAM2, CLIPSeg) along with custom expert vision models to automatically annotate large volumes of field imagery. Through active learning, the pipeline selects and down-samples the most informative samples, which are then reviewed and refined by human annotators before being passed downstream into the model training workflow. * **Cloud native ML Pipeline:** Aigen accelerates model training on Amazon SageMaker AI, using Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) across multi-GPU clusters to achieve faster iteration cycles and efficient hyperparameter tuning. By scaling training in the cloud, Aigen...
